did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically AlexNet, GoogleNet, and ResNet, along with an ensemble model, to classify skin lesions (malignant melanoma, melanocytic nevus, and seborrheic keratosis) from dermoscopic images acquired via a smartphone device and a conventional dermatoscope.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the acquisition of dermoscopic images using a smartphone device (NurugoTM) and a conventional dermatoscope, followed by classification using Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet, GoogleNet, ResNet) and an ensemble model. It includes specific Methods, Results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity metrics), and a Discussion of new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: The Role in Teledermoscopy of an Inexpensive and Easy-to-Use Smartphone Device for the Classification of Three Types of Skin Lesions Using Convolutional Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic validity of an inexpensive smartphone microscope (NurugoTM) for teledermoscopy by classifying malignant melanoma, melanocytic nevus, and seborrheic keratosis using convolutional neural networks (CNNs). The CNNs, including AlexNet, GoogleNet, ResNet, and an ensemble model, were trained on 600 images from the ISIC archive and tested on ISIC images, conventional dermatoscope images, and NurugoTM images. The ensemble model achieved up to 80% accuracy on the NurugoTM test set, with only two misclassifications out of ten images, demonstrating comparable results to clinical dermatoscopes despite device limitations. The results suggest that the low-cost NurugoTM device can provide reasonable diagnostic accuracy for skin lesion classification. Consequently, the device may serve as a useful triage tool for general practitioners to identify lesions requiring face-to-face dermatological consultation.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary classes used in the CNN classification task. Considered as true positives for performance evaluation metrics.
One of the three primary classes used in the CNN classification task. Considered as true negatives for performance evaluation metrics.
One of the three primary classes used in the CNN classification task. Considered as true negatives for performance evaluation metrics.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions: malignant melanoma (MM), melanocytic nevus (MN), and seborrheic keratosis (SK).
all_task_types.details: The study employs Convolutional Neural Networks (AlexNet, GoogleNet, ResNet) and an ensemble model to classify dermoscopic images into three discrete classes (MM, MN, SK). The final predicted label is determined by the class with the maximum probability from the ensemble of output probabilities. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive/negative predictive values, and F1 score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (MSK ISIC subset)
datasets_used.description: An open-source collection of dermatological images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration archive. A specific subset (MSK ISIC) was used, filtered for images with resolution and dimensions similar to the smartphone microscope and cropped with a 3 mm FOV radius, specifically those presenting a visual ruler.

use.training: Used to train the CNN models (AlexNet, GoogleNet, ResNet) via transfer learning.
use.testing: A distinct subset of ISIC images (Test Set 1) was used to test the model's performance on ISIC images.

datasets_used.name: Conventional Dermatoscope Images (Test Set 2)
datasets_used.description: Images of skin lesions acquired at the Dermatologic Clinic (Maggiore della Carità Hospital, University of Eastern Piedmont, Novara) using a conventional contact dermatoscope. These images were cropped and rescaled to match the field of view of the smartphone microscope images.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the diagnostic validity of images acquired with a conventional dermatoscope compared to the smartphone device.

datasets_used.name: NurugoTM Derma Images (Test Set 3)
datasets_used.description: Images of the same skin lesions acquired using the inexpensive smartphone microscope device (NurugoTM Derma), both with and without a laboratory glass slide. These images were acquired at the Dermatologic Clinic (Maggiore della Carità Hospital, University of Eastern Piedmont, Novara).

use.testing: Used to evaluate the diagnostic validity of images acquired with the NurugoTM smartphone device.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the classification methods across different test sets.
To quantify the overall diagnostic ability of each classification method derived from the ROC analysis.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of performance metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, PV+, PV-, F1 score)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the diagnostic validity of automated algorithms (CNNs, ensemble, texture analysis) against expert dermatologists across different image sources (ISIC, conventional dermatoscope, NurugoTM).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between automated algorithms and dermatologists were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PV+, PV-, F1 score) and ROC/AUC analysis without statistical significance testing.

Test Set 1 (ISIC Images).description: Images from MSK ISIC archive, different from training set. Total 97 images (35 MN, 25 MM, 37 SK).
Test Set 1 (ISIC Images).notes: The ensemble model showed better levels of accuracy, sensitivity, and F1 score than individual CNNs (except GoogleNet in sensitivity) and dermatologists. All CNNs were in line with experts; ensemble exceeded them in sensitivity, overall accuracy, PV-, and F1 score.

Test Set 2 (Conventional Dermatoscope Im.description: Images of skin lesions acquired with a contact dermatoscope. Total 78 images (39 MN, 18 MM, 21 SK).
Test Set 2 (Conventional Dermatoscope Im.notes: Dermatologists' performance was overall higher compared to all automated methods. ResNet proved to be more stable with a higher F1 score than all models and specificity in line with experts. GoogleNet showed the highest sensitivity among CNNs. Some dermatoscope images looked familiar to experts, potentially biasing results.

Test Set 3 (NurugoTM Derma Images).description: Images acquired with Nurugo Derma smartphone device. Total 78 images (39 MN, 18 MM, 21 SK).
Test Set 3 (NurugoTM Derma Images).notes: The ensemble model reached an overall accuracy of 83.9%, sensitivity of 72.4%, specificity of 97.3%, and F1 score of 80.8%. Individual CNNs showed promising specificity but failed to rival experts in accuracy and sensitivity. The ensemble model balanced gaps of individual CNNs, showing performance in line with experts in accuracy and sensitivity, and scoring higher in specificity. Dermatologists were slightly worse than in Test Set 2 due to device artifacts (bubbles, lack of focus, streaks).

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble model combining AlexNet, GoogleNet, and ResNet
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning applied using pretrained models from the literature (AlexNet [15], GoogleNet [16], ResNet [17]); trained on MSK ISIC subset from ISIC archive
architecture_used.input_size: Images cropped with a 3 mm FOV radius; resolution and dimensions similar to smartphone microscope images

architecture_details.AlexNet: Employed a series of convolutional layers to extract higher-level representation of image content
architecture_details.GoogleNet: Organized to concatenate convolutional layers having different kernel sizes
architecture_details.ResNet: Adopted skip connections and batch normalization to perform the classification task
architecture_details.Ensemble Model: Combined predictions of the three deep networks; probability obtained as the average of the three output probabilities from each single CNN; final predicted label equal to the class with maximum probability over all classes (MM, MN, SK)

training_details.training_set: 600 images from MSK ISIC archive (200 Malignant Melanoma, 200 Melanocytic Nevus, 200 Seborrheic Keratosis)
training_details.validation: Validation-set images used to test learning level during training phase
training_details.testing: Tested on three test sets: ISIC images (Test Set 1), conventional dermatoscope images (Test Set 2), and NurugoTM images (Test Set 3)

classification_or_output_head.output_method: Average of output probabilities from individual CNNs for ensemble; maximum probability class selection for final label

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Texture analysis with K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classifier
preprocessing_techniques: Cropping: Images were cropped to have the same field of view (FOV) as the smartphone microscope images (circular area of 3 mm radius).
Rescaling: Images were rescaled to match the dimensions and resolution of the smartphone microscope images.
Segmentation: A segmentation algorithm based on the circular Hough transform was developed to circumscribe the portion of smartphone microscope images not contaminated by glare reflection artifacts.
Artifact handling: The study notes limitations due to glare, shadow, and bubbles, but does not report specific algorithmic removal techniques beyond the Hough transform segmentation for glare.
conclusions: The ensemble model combining AlexNet, GoogleNet, and ResNet achieved up to 80% accuracy (specifically 83.9% on NurugoTM images) in classifying malignant melanoma, melanocytic nevus, and seborrheic keratosis, demonstrating comparable diagnostic validity to clinical dermatoscopes despite device limitations.
The low-cost and easy-to-use NurugoTM smartphone microscope device can provide reasonable levels of diagnostic accuracy for skin lesion classification.
The NurugoTM device has the potential to serve as a useful triage tool for general practitioners to identify lesions requiring face-to-face dermatological consultation.

Artifacts such as air bubbles, lack of focus, and streaks caused by the smartphone device and laboratory slide represented a limitation factor for both deep learning algorithms and clinicians, reducing performance compared to conventional dermatoscope images.
The study was limited by a small dataset size and the specific constraints of the amateur smartphone device.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small dataset size (600 training images) and the use of a single subset (MSK ISIC) filtered for specific resolution and visual ruler presence, potentially introducing selection bias.
The NurugoTM smartphone device is intended for non-medical use; validation for clinical diagnostic purposes requires further studies.

Image acquisition with the NurugoTM device required a laboratory glass slide, which introduced significant artifacts including air bubbles, lack of focus, and streaks, negatively impacting both CNN and dermatologist performance.
The field of view (FOV) was excessively restricted by the glass slide artifact, limiting the amount of clinical context available for analysis.

In Test Set 2 (conventional dermatoscope), dermatologists may have been biased because they had previously observed the clinical appearance of the lesions before surgical excision, potentially inflating their performance relative to the CNNs.

The study did not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values); comparisons between automated algorithms and dermatologists relied on descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.).

The CNN models were trained on a limited dataset from the ISIC archive, which may not represent the full spectrum of human population and clinical presentations, as noted in the discussion referencing other studies.

The NurugoTM device's current limitations (need for lab slide, time-consuming acquisition, image artifacts) hinder its immediate deployment as a reliable diagnostic tool, though it shows promise for triage.
The study only evaluated three specific lesion types (malignant melanoma, melanocytic nevus, seborrheic keratosis), limiting the generalizability of the findings to other skin pathologies.
